critic_raw:
This paper investigates how seed lexicons used in NLP bias measurement exhibit their own biases and affect measurement stability. Several methodological concerns apply.

The paper uses WEAT and PCA as bias subspace metrics. WEAT requires a null permutation test over word association scores, and its p-values are sensitive to the size and coverage of the seed sets. Using the same seed sets both to define the bias subspace and to measure stability against that same subspace is circular: any seed set that spans a larger portion of the embedding manifold will produce larger measured bias even if the underlying representation has not changed. The paper appears to acknowledge this conceptually, but the experimental design needs a held-out evaluation paradigm where the measured bias is validated against an independent criterion (e.g., downstream task performance on a known-biased task) rather than comparing one seed set's measurement against another.

The use of PCA to characterize bias subspaces conflates variance explained with bias magnitude. PCA's principal components are ordered by variance, not by association with the protected attribute. A component capturing stylistic variation (formal vs. informal register) may explain more variance than a component capturing gender associations, and PCA will rank the former first. The claim that PCA captures 'differences represented by seed set pairs' is true in a mathematical sense but not in a bias-relevant sense unless additional verification ties the identified component to the attribute of interest.

The literature review methodology for collecting seed sets is not described with sufficient precision for replication. Key questions include: what search strategy was used, what databases were searched, what inclusion/exclusion criteria applied, and whether the curation was done by one or multiple reviewers. If the collected seed sets are not a representative or exhaustive sample of those actually used in the literature, the conclusions about 'best practices' are recommendations based on a potentially biased convenience sample.

Term frequency is cited as a feature of seed sets affecting stability. However, term frequency in the embedding training corpus is a confounder with embedding quality, not a property of the seed set per se. A word that appears rarely in training will have a poorly estimated embedding regardless of whether it is a good conceptual choice for the seed set. The paper needs to disentangle seed set conceptual validity from embedding estimation variance before attributing instability to seed selection.
all_issues_raised: WEAT measurements evaluated against the same seed set that defines the bias subspace are partially circular: seeds spanning more of the embedding manifold will produce larger measured bias by construction, not because the underlying bias is larger. An independent validation criterion is needed.

PCA identifies components by variance explained, not by association with the protected attribute. Components capturing stylistic or topical variation may outrank gender or race components; equating PCA subspaces with bias subspaces requires explicit validation of which component corresponds to the target attribute.

The literature review methodology for seed set collection is underdescribed. Without explicit search strategy, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and inter-rater agreement, the sample of seed sets may be a biased convenience sample that undermines the generalizability of the best-practice recommendations.

Term frequency as a seed set feature confounds embedding estimation quality with conceptual validity of the seed. Instability attributed to 'low-frequency seeds' may reflect poor embedding estimates rather than a problem with seed selection per se.
all_issues_adjudicated: WEAT measurements evaluated against the same seed set that defines the bias subspace are partially circular: seeds spanning more of the embedding manifold will produce larger measured bias by construction, not because the underlying bias is larger. An independent validation criterion is needed.

PCA identifies components by variance explained, not by association with the protected attribute. Components capturing stylistic or topical variation may outrank gender or race components; equating PCA subspaces with bias subspaces requires explicit validation of which component corresponds to the target attribute.

The literature review methodology for seed set collection is underdescribed. Without explicit search strategy, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and inter-rater agreement, the sample of seed sets may be a biased convenience sample that undermines the generalizability of the best-practice recommendations.

Term frequency as a seed set feature confounds embedding estimation quality with conceptual validity of the seed. Instability attributed to 'low-frequency seeds' may reflect poor embedding estimates rather than a problem with seed selection per se.
